_comment: PHASE C WESTERN BREADTH - VICKSBURG, May-July 1863. A three-phase siege battle for the __FIELD real-time engine: May 19 Stockade Redan, May 22 grand assault (Jackson Road front), and the June siege approaches / mine against the 3rd Louisiana Redan. The teaching point is architectural: Vicksburg was not a single field battle but a fortress system of loess ravines, ridge roads, redans, lunettes, artillery batteries, covered approaches, mines, and collapsing logistics. Strengths are Inferred brigade-scale approximations; the phase sequence, principal ground, siege works, failed assaults, and Union engineering pressure are the teaching-bearing facts.

weather.note: Mississippi's hot, dry season — searing heat and scarce water drove Grant's May assaults; dust and powder-smoke hung over the lines.
sources: Ulysses S. Grant, Personal Memoirs of U. S. Grant, Vol. II, Ch. 55: 'We were in a Southern climate, at the beginning of the hot season... under a tropical sun' — heat and scarce water among his stated reasons to assault May 22, 1863
National Park Service, Vicksburg National Military Park — 'Vicksburg Siege' history page (May 22 simultaneous assault at 10 a.m.; June 25 Third Louisiana Redan mine ~3:30 p.m.)
American Battlefield Trust — 'Vicksburg' battle page and 'Vicksburg, May 22, 1863' map (10 a.m. attack on a three-mile front)

vicksburg.name: Vicksburg - Siege of the River Fortress
vicksburg.date: May 18-July 4, 1863 (invested May 18; first assault May 19; surrender July 4)
vicksburg.place: Vicksburg, Warren County, Mississippi - the bluffs above the Mississippi River
vicksburg.blurb: Grant's army has crossed below Vicksburg, won the inland campaign, and driven Pemberton behind the city's earthworks. The fortress is a six-and-a-half-mile arc of redans, redoubts, lunettes, batteries, ridge roads, and ravines, with both flanks resting on the Mississippi. The May assaults fail against the parapets; the siege is won by the spade, the sap, the mine, and the naval-battery pressure that makes surrender inevitable.
vicksburg.provenance: Verified (high) for the siege sequence, the May 19 and May 22 assaults, the Stockade Redan / 3rd Louisiana Redan / Great Redoubt / Railroad Redoubt, Battery DeGolyer, the covered approaches and mines, the July 4 surrender, and the strategic result splitting the Confederacy at the Mississippi. Inferred (medium) for per-unit strengths and exact sector timing. Anti-Lost-Cause frame: Vicksburg fell because Union maneuver isolated it and Union engineering dug it out; hunger mattered, but the siege architecture and campaign logistics made surrender unavoidable.

menu.title: VICKSBURG (1863)
menu.deck: The river fortress - a three-phase Western siege. Test the May assaults against the redans, then drive the saps, batteries, and mine that make the city untenable.
menu.aria: Vicksburg, May to July 1863 - a multi-phase Western Theater siege battle: Stockade Redan, the May 22 grand assault against the Great Redoubt, and the siege approaches and mine.

sides.heading: Vicksburg - Take Which Command?
sides.intro: May-July 1863 - the Mississippi fortress. Three siege sectors, fought in sequence. Lead the army of your choosing.

US.title: Command the Union (Grant's army)
US.deck: Probe the ridge-gates, survive the failed assaults, and then win as Grant did: batteries, covered approaches, mines, and relentless logistics.

CS.title: Command the Confederacy (Pemberton's garrison)
CS.deck: Hold the redans, protect the ridge roads, and keep the garrison alive long enough to defy Grant's siege lines.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. Three phases play in sequence; the cost carries forward (press V in battle to toggle fog).

brief.attack: This is not one charge across one field. First test the Stockade Redan, then the May 22 assault at the Great Redoubt, then turn to the spade: saps, batteries, and the mine. This is your war: Vicksburg is dug out, not simply starved out.
brief.defend: Hold the gates of the fortress: the Stockade Redan, the 3rd Louisiana Redan, the Great Redoubt, and the cratered line. This is your war: keep the ridge roads shut and make Grant pay for every yard.

phases.name: May 19 - Stockade Redan
phases.sector: the northern ridge gate - Graveyard Road and the Stockade Redan

transition.title: Stockade Redan
transition.lead: May 19. Grant tries a quick assault before Pemberton's shaken army can settle. Sherman's men climb the ravines toward the Stockade Redan on Graveyard Road - and discover that the loess ridges turn every road into a defended throat.

hills.name: the loess ridge before Graveyard Road
hills.note: Wind-cut yellow loess made narrow ridges and steep ravines around Vicksburg. Every approach road climbed a defended spine. (Verified)

hills.name: the Stockade Redan parapet
hills.note: The northern earthwork astride Graveyard Road, one of the first assault targets on May 19. (Verified)

hills.name: Fort Hill / river flank
hills.note: The northern river-facing flank of the Vicksburg works, tying the line to the Mississippi. (Verified)

woods.note: Ravine timber screening the Union approach before the slope opens under fire.
The broken side-ravines that made units lose alignment before reaching the parapet.

walls.note: STOCKADE REDAN PARAPET - the earthwork lip covering Graveyard Road. This is a siege wall, not open-field cover. (Verified)
ABATIS AND RIFLE PITS - forward obstacles below the redan that slow the assault and keep the attackers under musketry. (Inferred placement)

markers.name: Graveyard Road
markers.note: The northern road into Vicksburg and the axis of Sherman's May 19 assault. (Verified)

markers.name: Stockade Redan
markers.note: A redan is an angled earthwork projecting from the main line; this one guarded Graveyard Road.

markers.name: loess ravines
markers.note: The maze-like terrain that broke assault columns before they reached the works.

markers.name: Mississippi River flank
markers.note: Both ends of Pemberton's line rested near the river bluffs, reducing the chance of a simple flank attack.

leaders._note: The first assault tests the works before the siege has fully tightened. Grant and Sherman press quickly; Pemberton and Hebert hold the northern line.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Ulysses S. Grant
US.note: Army commander. Grant tried the May 19 assault hoping to exploit Confederate disorganization after Champion Hill and Big Black River. When it failed, he continued probing rather than withdrawing. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. William T. Sherman
US.note: Sherman's corps attacked the northern approaches, including the Stockade Redan sector. (Verified)

CS.name: Lt. Gen. John C. Pemberton
CS.note: Commander of the Vicksburg garrison. He had lost the field campaign, but his engineers gave him a formidable defensive shell. (Verified)

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Martin L. Smith
CS.note: Smith's division held the northern Vicksburg sector astride Graveyard Road, including the Stockade Redan; Shoup's and Baldwin's brigades manned the works; Bowen's Missourians came up as the mobile reserve that broke Sherman's May 19 assault. (Verified at division-sector level; brigade-scale placement Inferred. Rank corrected: BRIG. GEN. for the entire siege; his major-generalcy is dated Nov 4, 1863, four months AFTER the July 4 surrender, while he was a prisoner of war.)

US.name: Union ordnance park - the approach roads
Sherman's Assault Column
US.note: Sherman's corps carried the northern assault, climbing the Graveyard Road ravines into converging fire. Strength is brigade-scale abstraction. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Blair's Division
US.note: Union infantry pressing the Graveyard Road ravines toward the redan, exposed on the open approach. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Approach Batteries
US.note: Only light field batteries supported the May 19 rush; the siege artillery park was not yet built, and guns could not reduce the earthwork. Universal gun model: 4 guns. (Inferred)

CS.name: Smith's Division - Stockade Redan Defense
CS.note: Smith's division (Shoup's and Baldwin's brigades) behind the Stockade Redan parapet, with Bowen's Missouri reserves counterattacking in support; the works multiply their power and break the exposed assault. (Verified division-sector; Inferred strength. Rank: BRIG. GEN. - his MG dated Nov 1863, after the surrender.)

CS.name: Fort Hill Detachment
CS.note: Northern flank troops tying the redan sector to the river-facing works. (Inferred)

CS.name: Redan Batteries
CS.note: Pre-sited fortress guns firing from and behind the parapet into the Graveyard Road approach - the defender's fire edge in the May assault. Universal gun model: 9 guns. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: Ewing's Support Line
reinforcements.note: Additional Union infantry fed into the northern assault after the lead line stalls - and into the same converging fire. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: Interior Reserve
reinforcements.note: The defenders can shift short interior reserves behind the works faster than the attacker can climb the ravines. (Inferred)

teaching.head: The first assault meets the architecture
teaching.body: The May 19 attack proves that Vicksburg is not just a city on a bluff. The road into it is a defended ridge throat, walled by ravines and capped by an angled earthwork. The Confederates are not superhuman; the geometry is doing work for them. Grant learns the lesson and keeps tightening the ring.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) for the May 19 assault and Stockade Redan sector; exact tactical strengths inferred.

phases.name: May 22 - The Grand Assault
phases.sector: the May 22 assault front - 3rd Louisiana Redan and Great Redoubt astride the Jackson Road, with the Railroad Redoubt in S.D. Lee's sector to the south

transition.title: The Grand Assault
transition.lead: May 22. Grant orders a general assault along the whole line. On Graveyard Road to the north, Sherman's 150 unmarried volunteers - the 'forlorn hope' - carry boards and ladders against the Stockade Redan. Here on the Jackson Road front, Logan's division of McPherson's XVII Corps storms the 3rd Louisiana Redan and Great Redoubt, while McClernand's men briefly plant colors on the Railroad Redoubt to the south. The works are reached in places, but the line does not break.

hills.name: 3rd Louisiana Redan
hills.note: Major earthwork in the Jackson Road sector, later targeted by mines. (Verified)

hills.name: Great Redoubt
hills.note: Large work near the Jackson Road approach, one of the key May 22 assault points. (Verified)

hills.name: Railroad Redoubt
hills.note: The rail gap sector briefly penetrated during the May 22 assault before Confederate counteraction. (Verified)

woods.note: Broken ravines near the railroad approach.
Ravines below the Great Redoubt and Shirley House.

walls.note: MAIN CONFEDERATE EARTHWORK LINE - 3rd Louisiana Redan, Great Redoubt, and adjoining rifle pits. (Verified)

DITCH AND OBSTACLE LINE - every assault column had to cross this killing strip; on Graveyard Road the forlorn hope carried boards and ladders for the same problem. (Verified concept; inferred placement)

markers.name: Jackson Road
markers.note: The approach toward the 3rd Louisiana Redan and Great Redoubt, past the Shirley House. (Verified)

markers.name: Southern Railroad cut
markers.note: The railroad gap and nearby redoubt were among the most important openings in the line. (Verified)

markers.name: The ditch - the killing strip
markers.note: Attackers had to bridge the ditch and scale the parapet under fire. The famous 150-volunteer forlorn hope did this on Graveyard Road against the Stockade Redan, farther north on Sherman's front. (Verified)

markers.name: Shirley House
markers.note: A landmark near the Jackson Road siege approaches and batteries. (Verified)

markers.name: Battery DeGolyer
markers.note: Union siege battery near the Jackson Road sector. (Verified)

leaders._note: The second assault is broader and bloodier. McClernand reports success too generously; Grant refuses to keep feeding the army against unbroken works after this day.

US.name: Maj. Gen. John A. McClernand
US.note: Commanded XIII Corps. His May 22 reports helped convince Grant to renew pressure, but the line did not actually break. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. John A. Logan
US.note: Commanded a division in McPherson's XVII Corps and later pushed siege approaches toward the 3rd Louisiana Redan. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. James B. McPherson
US.note: XVII Corps commander, Grant's trusted engineer-soldier. His front worked the Jackson Road sector hard. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. John H. Forney
CS.note: Forney's division held much of the central Vicksburg line - the Jackson Road sector with the 3rd Louisiana Redan and the Great Redoubt, the sectors later mined. (Verified sector)

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Louis Hebert
CS.note: Hebert's brigade (Forney's division) held the 3rd Louisiana Redan - the work named for his old regiment - against the May 22 assault and the later June 25 mine. This is his historical sector. (Verified)

CS.name: Brig. Gen. John S. Bowen
CS.note: One of Pemberton's most capable subordinates. Bowen had fought well in the campaign before the army was forced inside Vicksburg. (Verified - rank corrected: BRIG. GEN. on the May 22 assault date; his major-generalcy is dated May 25, 1863, three days later, and was never confirmed by the Confederate Congress.)

US.name: Logan's Assault Column
US.note: Logan's division of McPherson's XVII Corps led the May 22 assault on this front, astride the Jackson Road against the Great Redoubt and 3rd Louisiana Redan, abstracted as a lead assault brigade. The 150-volunteer 'forlorn hope' storming party led Sherman's Stockade Redan assault farther north on Graveyard Road. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Logan's Division
US.note: XVII Corps pressure in the Jackson Road sector, advancing across the open ground before the redans. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: McClernand's XIII Corps Column
US.note: Part of the general assault of May 22, exposed on the approach to the Railroad Redoubt. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Battery DeGolyer
US.note: Field-battery support for the May 22 assault; the heavy siege park came later. Universal gun model: 6 guns. (Verified landmark; Inferred battery grouping)

CS.name: 3rd Louisiana Redan Defense
CS.note: Hebert's brigade (Forney's division) in and around the 3rd Louisiana Redan, firing from the parapet into the May 22 assault. (Verified sector; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Great Redoubt Garrison
CS.note: Infantry holding the Great Redoubt and adjacent rifle pits. (Verified sector; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Railroad Redoubt Detachment
CS.note: Troops guarding the rail gap and redoubt sector, briefly penetrated on May 22 before a counterattack sealed it. (Verified sector; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Jackson Road Batteries
CS.note: Pre-sited fortress guns firing from the parapets and interior positions - the defender's fire edge over the open assault. Universal gun model: 11 guns. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: Second Wave with Ladders
reinforcements.entry: following the lead column into the ditch
reinforcements.note: The assault wave meant to exploit any breach opened by the lead volunteers - and to die in the same ditch. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: Counterattack Reserve
reinforcements.note: Short interior movement lets the defense seal a threatened point. (Inferred)

teaching.head: A storming party cannot solve a fortress
teaching.body: The Forlorn Hope shows courage, but courage is not engineering. Boards and ladders get men into the ditch; they do not erase a prepared earthwork, interlocking artillery, and interior reserves. After May 22, Grant turns fully to siege craft.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) for the May 22 assault and Forlorn Hope role; exact unit strengths inferred.

phases.name: The Saps and the Mine
phases.sector: the siege lines - Battery DeGolyer, Logan's approach, and the mined 3rd Louisiana Redan

transition.title: The Saps and the Mine
transition.lead: The assaults have failed. Now the siege speaks in yards: parallels, zig-zag saps, protected batteries, sharpshooter pits, and mine galleries creeping toward the redans. At the 3rd Louisiana Redan, Union soldiers tunnel under the parapet and blow a crater in the line.

hills.name: 3rd Louisiana Redan crater
hills.note: The mine under the 3rd Louisiana Redan exploded June 25; a second mine followed July 1. (Verified)

hills.name: Battery DeGolyer ridge
hills.note: Union siege batteries on the Jackson Road front pounded the works and protected the approach. (Verified)

hills.name: interior Vicksburg ridge
hills.note: The interior high ground and magazines behind the threatened redan. (Verified as strategic geography; exact field placement inferred)

woods.note: ravine cover on the left approach
ravine cover near the Jackson Road battery positions

walls.note: CRATERED CONFEDERATE PARAPET - the 3rd Louisiana Redan after mining, still dangerous but physically breached. (Verified)
UNION FIRST PARALLEL - protected trench line from which the saps push forward. (Verified concept; inferred placement)
ZIG-ZAG SAP - covered approach trench advancing toward the mine gallery. (Verified concept; inferred placement)

markers.name: Jackson Road
markers.note: The road and ridge line that focused Union siege work near the 3rd Louisiana Redan. (Verified)

markers.name: Logan's approach
markers.note: A covered approach toward the 3rd Louisiana Redan, representing the Union siege saps. (Verified concept)

markers.name: mine gallery
markers.note: Union miners tunneled under the redan and exploded a mine on June 25. (Verified)

markers.name: powder crater
markers.note: The blast opened a crater but did not instantly collapse the entire line; fighting continued around the breach.

markers.name: countermine galleries
markers.note: Defenders listened and dug countermines, another layer of siege architecture. (Verified concept)

markers.name: Battery DeGolyer
markers.note: A named Union battery site on the siege front. (Verified)

leaders._note: The final phase gives the Union the tools that made Vicksburg fall: engineering, siege guns, mines, logistics, and time.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Ulysses S. Grant
US.note: Grant accepts that direct assault is the wrong answer and uses the full machinery of siege war. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. John A. Logan
US.note: Logan's front pushed close against the 3rd Louisiana Redan and its mined sector. (Verified sector; exact game attachment inferred)

CS.name: Lt. Gen. John C. Pemberton
CS.note: Pemberton's army is trapped, short of supplies, and under constant artillery and mining pressure. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. John H. Forney
CS.note: Forney's men held part of the mined central front. (Verified sector; exact game attachment inferred)

US.name: Union siege depot - roads to the Yazoo and river batteries
Logan's Siege Approach
US.note: Infantry and pioneers pushing protected approaches toward the 3rd Louisiana Redan. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Engineer and Miner Details
US.note: The siege is won by technical work: trenching, mining, revetments, and battery protection - not by storming. (Verified role; Inferred strength)

US.name: Siege Batteries
US.note: The siege-battery abstraction: sustained fire protecting the saps and slowly battering the parapets. Universal gun model: 14 guns. (Inferred grouping)

CS.name: Forney's Crater Line
CS.note: Hebert's and Forney's exhausted defenders holding the mined 3rd Louisiana Redan, dug in behind the cratered parapet. (Verified sector; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Countermine Detail
CS.note: Defenders trying to detect, counter-mine, and seal Union tunnel work. (Verified concept; Inferred strength)

CS.name: Worn Confederate Batteries
CS.note: The garrison guns are fewer, worn, and short of ammunition as the siege closes. Universal gun model: 6 guns. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: Mine Assault Party
reinforcements.entry: through the covered approach after the blast
reinforcements.note: Infantry moving through the sap toward the crater after the mine explosion. (Inferred)

reinforcements.name: River Battery Pressure
reinforcements.entry: naval and river-facing pressure tightening the fortress
reinforcements.note: Abstracts the wider Union fire pressure from river and siege batteries. Universal gun model: 6 guns. (Verified effect; Inferred field placement)

reinforcements.name: Last Interior Reserve
reinforcements.entry: from the inner streets and magazines
reinforcements.note: By late June, the defense can still counterattack locally, but every reserve is smaller and hungrier. (Inferred)

teaching.head: The siege is a machine
teaching.body: This is the phase that makes Vicksburg Vicksburg: batteries pin the parapets, saps shorten the killing ground, mines break the geometry, and logistics squeeze the garrison. Hunger is real, but it is the result of operational isolation plus siege architecture, not a substitute for it.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) for the mines and siege approaches; exact field abstraction inferred.

US.US: History holds. Vicksburg falls because the field campaign isolated it and the siege works made continued defense impossible. The Mississippi is opened from Minnesota to the Gulf, the Confederacy is cut in two, and Grant's reputation becomes national.
US.CS: History holds against you. The garrison held the parapets in May, but the siege machine kept coming: batteries, saps, mines, and hunger. Vicksburg surrenders on July 4, 1863, and the Mississippi is lost.

CS.US: History is overturned. Pemberton's garrison has kept the ridge gates shut and survived the mining pressure longer than history allowed. The Mississippi remains contested, and Grant's western victory is delayed.
CS.CS: History is overturned. Your garrison holds the redans, checks the saps, and keeps Vicksburg alive past the historical breaking point. The river fortress still bars the Mississippi.

endNote.draw: The fortress is neither taken nor saved. Some sectors held and some gave way, the siege approaches still crawl forward, and the fate of the Mississippi hangs on the next week.

teaching.title: Vicksburg - Dug Out of the Bluffs

cards.head: The river was the strategic prize
cards.body: Vicksburg controlled the Mississippi bend from high bluffs. When it fell, the Confederacy lost its last major hold on the river and the Trans-Mississippi was effectively severed from the eastern Confederacy. Gettysburg and Vicksburg landed in the same week; one broke Lee's offensive power, the other broke the Confederate map.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - campaign sequence and strategic result.

cards.head: The May assaults failed for architectural reasons
cards.body: The defenders fought from redans, redoubts, rifle pits, and batteries placed on narrow ridge roads above ravines. The first lesson is not romance; it is geometry. Attackers had to climb broken ground, cross obstacles, and arrive disordered under converging fire.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - Stockade Redan, 3rd Louisiana Redan, Great Redoubt, and Railroad Redoubt sectors.

cards.head: Grant turned to the spade
cards.body: After May 22, the battle becomes engineering. Union troops dug parallels and zig-zag saps, built batteries, pushed mines under the works, and reduced the distance to the parapets yard by yard. Vicksburg was dug out, not merely waited out.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - siege approaches and mines against the 3rd Louisiana Redan.

cards.head: Pemberton's trap was operational
cards.body: Pemberton did not lose Vicksburg only at the parapet. He was beaten in the campaign before the siege: Grant crossed below the city, moved inland, won at Champion Hill and Big Black River, and drove the army into a fortress with no open road out.

cards.head: The river war also changed slavery's geography
cards.body: Union control of the Mississippi turned movement, labor, and information against the slaveholding war effort. Camps, levees, military labor, and flight to Union lines made the river corridor a corridor of emancipation as well as supply. That agency included combat: at Milliken's Bend on June 7, 1863, newly raised Black regiments - the 9th and 11th Louisiana and 1st Mississippi (African Descent) - held the riverbank in hand-to-hand fighting against a Confederate relief attack, one of the war's earliest proofs of Black soldiers in pitched battle.
cards.provenance: Verified (medium-high) - strategic river control and emancipation geography per NPS Vicksburg; Milliken's Bend USCT combat per NPS and American Battlefield Trust; tactical effects abstracted.

codex.title: Vicksburg, May 18-July 4, 1863
codex.summary: Grant's inland campaign drove Pemberton into Vicksburg, whose bluffs and earthworks barred the Mississippi. Direct assaults on May 19 and May 22 failed, especially at the Stockade Redan, 3rd Louisiana Redan, Great Redoubt, and Railroad Redoubt. Grant then settled into siege: batteries, saps, mines, and logistics broke the fortress. The July 4 surrender opened the Mississippi and split the Confederacy.
